### Remote connections

Source: [Remote connections](/codex/remote-connections.md)

import {
Desktop,
Storage,
Terminal,
} from "@components/react/oai/platform/ui/Icon.react";

Remote connections let you use Codex from another device or another machine.
Use Codex in the ChatGPT mobile app to work with Codex on a connected Mac or
Windows device, continue work from another supported Codex App device, or connect
the Codex App to projects on an SSH host.

Remote access uses the connected host's projects, threads, files, credentials,
permissions, plugins, Computer Use, browser setup, and local tools.

#### What you can do remotely

- Start new threads in projects on the host, or continue existing ones.
- Send follow-up instructions, answer questions, and steer active work.
- Approve commands and other actions.
- Review outputs, diffs, test results, terminal output, and screenshots.
- Get notified when Codex completes a task or needs your attention.
- Switch between connected hosts and threads.

The next sections cover using Codex in the ChatGPT mobile app to control a Codex
App host. To connect Codex to a project on an SSH host, see
[connect to an SSH host](#connect-to-an-ssh-host).

#### Before you set up mobile access

Codex mobile setup supports Codex App hosts on macOS and Windows. You can
control a Windows host from ChatGPT on iOS or Android, or from a Mac running
Codex. Windows can't currently control another computer from the Codex App.

Make sure you have:

- Codex access in the ChatGPT account and workspace you want to use.
- The latest ChatGPT mobile app on an iOS or Android device. If you don't see
  Codex in the ChatGPT mobile app, update ChatGPT first.
- The latest Codex App for macOS or Windows running on a host that's awake,
  online, and signed in to the same account and workspace. Mobile setup starts
  from the Codex App; you can't set it up from the Codex CLI or IDE Extension.
- Any required multi-factor authentication, SSO, or passkey configuration for
  that account or workspace.

If you use Codex through a ChatGPT workspace, your admin may need to enable
Remote Control access before you can connect from your phone.

#### Set up mobile access

Start in the Codex App on the host you want to connect. The setup flow enables
remote access for that host, then shows a QR code you can scan from your phone.
The QR code pairs that phone with that host. Pair every phone or supported Codex
App device with every host you want it to control.

Existing connections used since June 8, 2026, remain paired. If you haven't
used an existing connection since June 8, 2026, update both apps and pair the
devices again.

1. Start Codex mobile setup.

   Open Codex on the host and select **Set up Codex mobile** in the
   sidebar.

2. Scan the QR code.

   Use your phone to scan the QR code shown by Codex. The code opens ChatGPT so
   you can finish connecting the mobile app to the host.

3. Finish setup in ChatGPT.

   ChatGPT opens the Codex mobile setup flow. Confirm the same ChatGPT account
   and workspace, then complete any required multi-factor authentication, SSO,
   or passkey steps. After setup succeeds, the host appears in Codex on your
   phone.

4. Review host settings.

   In Codex on the host, use **Settings > Connections** to manage connected
   devices. You can also choose whether to keep the computer awake, enable
   Computer Use, or install the Chrome extension.

#### Choose what to connect

Start with the laptop or desktop where you already use Codex. Add an always-on
computer or SSH host when you need continuous access or a different environment.

#### Your laptop or desktop

Connect the Mac or Windows PC where you already run Codex day to day. This gives
remote access to the same projects, threads, credentials, plugins, and local
setup you already use.

If that computer sleeps, loses network access, or closes Codex, remote access
stops until it's available again. If you use this computer as your host device,
keep it plugged in and use the host's connection settings to keep it awake where
available.

On a Mac laptop, remote access can stay available with the lid open and power
connected. With the lid closed, connect an external display as well. Choosing
**Sleep** still stops remote access.

On a Windows host, keep the session unlocked and available for tasks that use
[Computer Use](/codex/app/computer-use). Computer use on Windows runs in the
foreground, so remote control is best for starting or checking work while you
dedicate the host desktop to the task.

#### A dedicated always-on computer

Use a dedicated always-on Mac or Windows PC when you want Codex to stay
reachable for longer-running work.

Install the projects, credentials, plugins, MCP servers, and tools Codex should
use on that machine.

#### A remote development environment

Use an SSH host or managed remote development environment when the project
already lives in a remote environment. Connect the Codex App host to that
environment first; your phone still connects to the Codex App host, and Codex
works in the remote environment with its dependencies, security policies, and
compute resources.

For SSH setup details, see [connect to an SSH host](#connect-to-an-ssh-host).

For browser or desktop tasks on an always-on computer or remote host, enable
Computer Use and install the Chrome extension on that host.

#### What comes from the connected host

Your phone sends prompts, approvals, and follow-up messages to Codex. The
connected host provides the environment Codex uses.

That means:

- Repository files and local documents come from the connected host.
- Shell commands run on that host or remote environment.
- Any plugin installed on that host is available when you use Codex remotely.
- MCP servers, skills, browser access, and Computer Use come from that host's
  configuration.
- Signed-in websites and desktop apps are available only when the host can
  access them.
- The sandboxing settings, security controls, and action approvals still apply
  to the connected session.

Codex uses a secure relay layer to keep trusted machines reachable across your
authorized ChatGPT devices without exposing them directly to the public
internet.

#### Pick up work from another device

You can continue work from another signed-in Codex App device that supports
remote control. For example, if your laptop is unavailable, you can start
a thread from your phone on an always-on host, then later open Codex on your
laptop and continue that same thread there.

In Codex on a Mac, use **Settings > Connections > Control other devices** to add
the other host. A device can allow remote access and control another device at
the same time. You can control Windows hosts from a Mac or from ChatGPT on iOS
or Android, but you can't use Windows to control another computer. For example,
you can control a Windows device from your Mac or phone, but you can't use a
Windows device to control another Windows device.

#### Connect to an SSH host

In the Codex App, add remote projects from an SSH host and run threads against
the remote filesystem and shell. Remote project threads run commands, read
files, and write changes on the remote host.

Keep the remote host configured with the same security expectations you use for
normal SSH access: trusted keys, least-privilege accounts, and no
unauthenticated public listeners.

1. Add the host to your SSH config so Codex can auto-discover it.

   ```text
   Host devbox
     HostName devbox.example.com
     User you
     IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
   ```

   Codex reads concrete host aliases from `~/.ssh/config`, resolves them with
   OpenSSH, and ignores pattern-only hosts.

2. Confirm you can SSH to the host from the machine running the Codex App.

   ```bash
   ssh devbox
   ```

3. Install and authenticate Codex on the remote host.

   The app starts the remote Codex app server through SSH, using the remote
   user's login shell. Make sure the `codex` command is available on the
   remote host's `PATH` in that shell.

4. In the Codex App, open **Settings > Connections**, add or enable the SSH
   host, then choose a remote project folder.

payload.output:
Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.2 seconds
Output:
Fork a previous interactive session (picker by default; use --last to fork the most recent)

Usage: codex fork [OPTIONS] [SESSION_ID] [PROMPT]

Arguments:
  [SESSION_ID]
          Conversation/session id (UUID). When provided, forks this session. If omitted, use --last
          to pick the most recent recorded session

  [PROMPT]
          Optional user prompt to start the session

Options:
  -c, --config <key=value>
          Override a configuration value that would otherwise be loaded from `~/.codex/config.toml`.
          Use a dotted path (`foo.bar.baz`) to override nested values. The `value` portion is parsed
          as TOML. If it fails to parse as TOML, the raw string is used as a literal.
          
          Examples: - `-c model="o3"` - `-c 'sandbox_permissions=["disk-full-read-access"]'` - `-c
          shell_environment_policy.inherit=all`

      --last
          Fork the most recent session without showing the picker

      --all
          Show all sessions (disables cwd filtering and shows CWD column)

      --enable <FEATURE>
          Enable a feature (repeatable). Equivalent to `-c features.<name>=true`

      --disable <FEATURE>
          Disable a feature (repeatable). Equivalent to `-c features.<name>=false`

      --remote <ADDR>
          Connect the TUI to a remote app server endpoint.
          
          Accepted forms: `ws://host:port`, `wss://host:port`, `unix://`, or `unix://PATH`.

      --remote-auth-token-env <ENV_VAR>
          Name of the environment variable containing the bearer token to send to a remote app
          server websocket

      --strict-config
          Error out when config.toml contains fields that are not recognized by this version of
          Codex

  -i, --image <FILE>...
          Optional image(s) to attach to the initial prompt

go through the [@linear](plugin://linear@openai-curated-remote) completed projects in the last day. if there are no completed projects in the last 24 hours, just stop. otherwise, for each completed project please go through the codex session logs that correspond to each completed project and update that project with the final state of how it was implemented. Shipped in GitHub PR #70: https://github.com/adamblumoff/scope-vcs/pull/70

Merged at `2026-06-29T17:35:13Z` via merge commit `ae76abf7a4e43665d6b15bac6c026c58a5244b2f` from branch `codex/access-control-collaboration`.

What landed:

- Added a durable repository collaboration model with `api/src/domain/repo_collaboration.rs` and `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` owning invite/member behavior instead of scattering rules through HTTP/Git code.
- Replaced the old role shape with per-repository access capabilities: read private files, push, change visibility, apply changes, update settings, manage members, and delete repo.
- Added persisted repository members and invites, including hashed invite tokens, expiry/state handling, verified-email matching on accept, and advisory-locked metadata writes.
- Added owner-only collaboration endpoints for listing members/invites, creating email invites, updating/removing members, reading invite tokens, and accepting invites.
- Updated Git/CLI access checks so members can read/push according to capability, while members without apply permission have pushes staged for owner review.
- Collapsed pending first-push/pending-publish lifecycle handling into the unpublished/published model with pending import state.
- Added the web member management and invite acceptance surfaces, including settings-page member controls, read-only member access display, and `/invites/:token` flow.
- Regenerated API/web contracts and route tree, updated repo events/live refresh behavior, and kept public/member/owner review access permission-aware.

Verification recorded on the PR:

- Local: `cargo test --manifest-path api/Cargo.toml`, `cargo check --manifest-path api/Cargo.toml --features local-dev`, `cargo test --manifest-path cli/Cargo.toml`, `pnpm check`, and `pnpm test`.
- Manual local dev browser check covered owner settings, published invite flow, unpublished invite blocking, invite landing page, and console errors.
- CI completed successfully for `Check scope-api`, `Check scope-cli`, `Check scope-web`, and all CLI binary builds: Linux x64, Linux ARM64, macOS Intel, macOS Apple Silicon, Windows x64, and Windows ARM64.

Notes:

- Local T3/Codex history matched the implementation primarily through git/PR metadata and the Codex session index. The direct access-control rollout summary referenced by T3 was not present in `~/.codex/memories/rollout_summaries` at automation time.
- The attached Linear issues were `ADA-26`, `ADA-27`, `ADA-30`, and `ADA-31`; Linear still showed them as unstarted/backlog when this final project update was written, so this update records project implementation state rather than mutating individual issue states."},"status":"completed","durationMs":1063},{"type":"agentMessage","id":"item-23","text":"Linear accepted the project status update as `3836a00f-3620-4a6f-99b4-51724aeaa261`.   <input>You own PR 4 of a stacked repo-wide Scope VCS change.

Repo/user instructions to obey:
- This product is pre-alpha. Prefer destructive simplification over backwards compatibility.
- No fake legacy adapters, no compatibility layers, no demo hardcoding.
- Do not add Railway config if CLI cannot do deployment work; explain dashboard steps instead.
- Avoid card-heavy UI; use cards only if absolutely necessary.
- Use existing project patterns, but simplify aggressively where code is horizontally spread without real value.

Stack order:
1. scope/01-real-app-domain
2. scope/02-shoo-auth
3. scope/03-dogfood-scope-vcs
4. scope/04-simplify-core (this thread)
5. scope/05-simplify-web, based on PR 4

Your branch: scope/04-simplify-core
Your intended base: scope/03-dogfood-scope-vcs. If that branch does not exist or is not ready, do an audit/prep pass only and stop with a clear waiting report rather than implementing against the wrong base.

Objective:
Simplify the Rust backend/core after the real app/auth/dogfooding slices exist. Collapse tiny/placeholder crates where they create horizontal spread without value. Bias toward a smaller vertical backend: server plus core modules for policy, projection, git projection, manifest/auth/store domain. Delete or absorb placeholder worker/audit/store/demo boundaries unless they are now clearly pulling their weight. Preserve behavior and tests from prior PRs, but do not add backwards compatibility for removed demo APIs.

Execution loop:
1. Check branch/worktree state first. Create/switch to scope/04-simplify-core from scope/03-dogfood-scope-vcs once available.
2. Implement a destructive simplification pass with tight scope.
3. Run a code-reaper pass: remove dead crates/files/exports, stale docs, duplicate paths, and any compatibility shims introduced only to preserve old demo shape.
4. Run formatting and focused tests, then full relevant checks: cargo fmt, cargo test --workspace, pnpm build if web imports changed.
5. Run autoreview as a closeout gate. Use the actual base ref once available, e.g. python .agents\skills\autoreview\scripts\autoreview --mode branch --base scope/03-dogfood-scope-vcs --parallel-tests-shell powershell --parallel-tests "cargo test --workspace"
6. For every accepted/actionable autoreview finding: verify it in the real code, fix only in-scope blockers, rerun focused tests, then rerun autoreview. Keep looping until autoreview exits clean with no accepted/actionable findings, or stop only if a finding clearly breaks this PR's scope and report it.
7. Commit only intended PR 4 changes. If you open a PR, make it a draft PR against scope/03-dogfood-scope-vcs.

Final report must include branch, files changed, tests run, autoreview command/result, accepted/rejected findings, and any remaining scope breaks.</input>

I’m testing the app-server proxy now so a scheduled service can create the same visible threads instead of using `codex exec`.

payload.message: I’m going to implement this as two pieces: a dispatcher that reads native `~/.codex/automations/*/automation.toml`, and a run worker that creates a real app-server thread. For `execution_environment = "worktree"` it will create a Codex-managed Git worktree under `$CODEX_HOME/worktrees`; for `local` it will use the configured checkout directly.

content.text: I’m going to implement this as two pieces: a dispatcher that reads native `~/.codex/automations/*/automation.toml`, and a run worker that creates a real app-server thread. For `execution_environment = "worktree"` it will create a Codex-managed Git worktree under `$CODEX_HOME/worktrees`; for `local` it will use the configured checkout directly.

payload.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /home/adam-blumoff/.local/bin/codex-app-automation-dispatch
+#!/usr/bin/env python3
+"""Dispatch due Codex app automation runs from ~/.codex/automations."""
+
+from __future__ import annotations
+
+import fcntl
+import json
+import os
+import subprocess
+import sys
+import tomllib
+from datetime import datetime
+from pathlib import Path
+
+
+HOME = Path.home()
+CODEX_HOME = Path(os.environ.get("CODEX_HOME", HOME / ".codex")).expanduser()
+AUTOMATIONS_DIR = CODEX_HOME / "automations"
+STATE_PATH = AUTOMATIONS_DIR / ".scheduler-state.json"
+LOCK_PATH = AUTOMATIONS_DIR / ".scheduler.lock"
+
+WEEKDAYS = {
+    "MO": 0,
+    "TU": 1,
+    "WE": 2,
+    "TH": 3,
+    "FR": 4,
+    "SA": 5,
+    "SU": 6,
+}
+
+
+def load_state() -> dict:
+    if not STATE_PATH.exists():
+        return {"last_started": {}}
+    return json.loads(STATE_PATH.read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
+
+
+def save_state(state: dict) -> None:
+    tmp = STATE_PATH.with_suffix(".tmp")
+    tmp.write_text(json.dumps(state, indent=2, sort_keys=True) + "\n", encoding="utf-8")
+    tmp.replace(STATE_PATH)
+
+
+def parse_rrule(rrule: str) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
+    if rrule.startswith("RRULE:"):
+        rrule = rrule.removeprefix("RRULE:")
+    parts: dict[str, list[str]] = {}
+    for raw_part in rrule.split(";"):
+        if not raw_part or "=" not in raw_part:
+            continue
+        key, value = raw_part.split("=", 1)
+        parts[key.upper()] = [item for item in value.split(",") if item]
+    return parts
+
+
+def due_key(config: dict, now: datetime) -> str | None:
+    if config.get("kind") != "cron" or config.get("status") != "ACTIVE":
+        return None
+
+    parts = parse_rrule(str(config.get("rrule", "")))
+    freq = (parts.get("FREQ") or [""])[0]
+    hours = {int(value) for value in parts.get("BYHOUR", ["0"])}
+    minutes = {int(value) for value in parts.get("BYMINUTE", ["0"])}
+    if now.hour not in hours or now.minute not in minutes:
+        return None
+
+    if freq == "WEEKLY":
+        bydays = parts.get("BYDAY") or []
+        if bydays and now.weekday() not in {WEEKDAYS[day] for day in bydays if day in WEEKDAYS}:
+            return None
+    elif freq != "DAILY":
+        print(f"unsupported automation RRULE frequency {freq!r} for {config.get('id')}", file=sys.stderr)
+        return None
+
+    return now.strftime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M%z")
+
+
+def automation_configs() -> list[tuple[str, dict]]:
+    configs: list[tuple[str, dict]] = []
+    for path in sorted(AUTOMATIONS_DIR.glob("*/automation.toml")):
+        with path.open("rb") as config_file:
+            config = tomllib.load(config_file)
+        automation_id = str(config.get("id") or path.parent.name)
+        configs.append((automation_id, config))
+    return configs
+
+
+def escaped_unit(automation_id: str) -> str:
+    return subprocess.check_output(
+        [
+            "systemd-escape",
+            "--template=codex-app-automation-run@.service",
+            automation_id,
+        ],
+        text=True,
+    ).strip()
+
+
+def main() -> int:
+    now = datetime.now().astimezone()
+    AUTOMATIONS_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
+    with LOCK_PATH.open("w", encoding="utf-8") as lock_file:

+#!/usr/bin/env python3
+"""Create a visible Codex app thread for one automation run."""

+from __future__ import annotations

+import argparse
+import fcntl
+import json

+import sys
+import tomllib
+from datetime import datetime
+from pathlib import Path
+from typing import Any

+HOME = Path.home()
+CODEX_HOME = Path(os.environ.get("CODEX_HOME", HOME / ".codex")).expanduser()
+AUTOMATIONS_DIR = CODEX_HOME / "automations"
+
+
+class AppServerClient:
+    def __init__(self, log_file):
+        self.log_file = log_file
+        self.next_id = 1
+        self.process = subprocess.Popen(
+            ["codex", "app-server"],
+            stdin=subprocess.PIPE,
+            stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
+            stderr=subprocess.STDOUT,
+            text=True,
+            bufsize=1,
+            env=self._env(),
+        )
+
+    def _env(self) -> dict[str, str]:
+        env = os.environ.copy()
+        env["HOME"] = str(HOME)
+        env["CODEX_HOME"] = str(CODEX_HOME)
+        env.setdefault("T3CODE_HOME", str(HOME / ".t3"))
+        return env
+
+    def send(self, method: str, params: Any = None, request_id: int | None = None) -> int | None:
+        message: dict[str, Any] = {"method": method}
+        if request_id is not None:
+            message["id"] = request_id
+        if params is not None:
+            message["params"] = params
+        assert self.process.stdin is not None
+        encoded = json.dumps(message, separators=(",", ":"))
+        self.log_file.write(f"> {encoded}\n")
+        self.log_file.flush()
+        self.process.stdin.write(encoded + "\n")
+        self.process.stdin.flush()
+        return request_id
+
+    def request(self, method: str, params: Any = None) -> Any:
+        request_id = self.next_id
+        self.next_id += 1
+        self.send(method, params, request_id)
+        while True:
+            message = self.read_message()
+            if message.get("id") != request_id:
+                continue
+            if "error" in message:
+                raise RuntimeError(f"{method} failed: {message['error']}")
+            return message.get("result")
+
+    def read_message(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
+        assert self.process.stdout is not None
+        line = self.process.stdout.readline()
+        if not line:
+            code = self.process.poll()
+            raise RuntimeError(f"codex app-server exited while waiting for a message: {code}")
+        self.log_file.write(f"< {line}")
+        self.log_file.flush()
+        return json.loads(line)
+
+    def close(self) -> None:
+        if self.process.poll() is None:
+            self.process.terminate()
+            try:
+                self.process.wait(timeout=5)
+            except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
+                self.process.kill()
+
+
+def load_automation(automation_id: str) -> tuple[Path, dict]:
+    automation_dir = AUTOMATIONS_DIR / automation_id
+    config_path = automation_dir / "automation.toml"
+    if not config_path.exists():
+        raise SystemExit(f"automation config not found: {config_path}")
+    with config_path.open("rb") as config_file:
+        config = tomllib.load(config_file)

+        raise SystemExit(f"automation id mismatch for {config_path}")

+def git_output(args: list[str], cwd: Path) -> str:
+    return subprocess.check_output(["git", "-C", str(cwd), *args], text=True).strip()

+def is_git_repo(path: Path) -> bool:
+    return subprocess.run(
+        ["git", "-C", str(path), "rev-parse", "--is-inside-work-tree"],
+        stdout=subprocess.DEVNULL,
+        stderr=subprocess.DEVNULL,

+def copy_worktree_includes(source_root: Path, target_root: Path) -> None:
+    includes = []
+    include_file = source_root / ".worktreeinclude"
+    if include_file.exists():
+        includes.extend(
+            line.strip()
+            for line in include_file.read_text(encoding="utf-8").splitlines()
+            if line.strip() and not line.strip().startswith("#")
+        )
+    if (source_root / "AGENTS.override.md").exists():
+        includes.append("AGENTS.override.md")
+
+    for pattern in includes:
+        for source in source_root.glob(pattern):
+            if not source.exists():
+                continue
+            relative = source.relative_to(source_root)
+            target = target_root / relative
+            if target.exists() or source.is_symlink():
+                continue
+            target.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
+            if source.is_dir():
+                shutil.copytree(source, target, symlinks=False, dirs_exist_ok=False)
+            else:

+
+def create_managed_worktree(cwd: Path, automation_id: str) -> Path:
+    repo_root = Path(git_output(["rev-parse", "--show-toplevel"], cwd))

+    )
+    copy_worktree_includes(repo_root, worktree_root)

+
+def thread_cwd(cwd: Path, execution_environment: str, automation_id: str) -> Path:

+def build_prompt(automation_id: str, automation_dir: Path, config: dict, cwd: Path, now: datetime) -> str:

+    return f"""Run this Codex app automation now.
+
+Automation metadata:
+- id: {automation_id}
+- name: {config.get("name", automation_id)}
+- schedule: {config.get("rrule", "unspecified")}
+- execution_environment: {config.get("execution_environment", "local")}
+- scheduled/manual run time: {now.isoformat()}
+- configured project: {cwd}
+- automation memory file: {memory_path}
+
+Automation prompt:
+<automation_prompt>
+{config.get("prompt", "")}
+</automation_prompt>
+
+Prior automation memory:
+<automation_memory>
+{memory}
+</automation_memory>

+Complete this run in this visible Codex thread. Before the final response,
+update the automation memory file with a concise latest-run summary including
+the run time, important actions taken, verification, and any blockers or gaps
+that matter for the next run.
+"""
+
+
+def run_for_cwd(automation_id: str, automation_dir: Path, config: dict, cwd: Path, run_log: Path) -> str:
+    now = datetime.now().astimezone()
+    target_cwd = thread_cwd(cwd, str(config.get("execution_environment") or "local"), automation_id)
+    prompt = build_prompt(automation_id, automation_dir, config, cwd, now)
+
+    with run_log.open("a", encoding="utf-8") as log_file:
+        log_file.write(f"run started at {now.isoformat()} cwd={target_cwd}\n")
+        log_file.flush()
+        client = AppServerClient(log_file)
+        try:
+            client.request(
+                "initialize",
+                {
+                    "clientInfo": {
+                        "name": "codex_app_automation_scheduler",
+                        "title": "Codex App Automation Scheduler",
+                        "version": "0.1.0",
+                    },
+                    "capabilities": {"experimentalApi": True},
+                },
+            )
+            client.send("initialized", {})
+            thread_result = client.request(
+                "thread/start",
+                {
+                    "cwd": str(target_cwd),
+                    "model": config.get("model"),
+                    "approvalPolicy": "never",
+                    "threadSource": "automation",
+                },
+            )
+            thread_id = thread_result["thread"]["id"]
+            client.request(
+                "thread/name/set",
+                {"threadId": thread_id, "name": f"Automation: {config.get('name', automation_id)}"},
+            )
+            client.request(

+                    "approvalPolicy": "never",
+                },

+def wait_for_completion(client: AppServerClient, thread_id: str) -> None:

Current concern:
The collaboration feature is implemented and tested, but its delivery/persistence surface is spread across broad files and repeats the same orchestration shape. `api/src/http/repos.rs` owns repo settings, file visibility, collaboration list/create/update/delete, invite lookup/accept, repeated owner/access checks, metadata mutation calls, second repo reads, change publication, and response hydration. `api/src/http/responses.rs` is also carrying collaboration DTOs and response helpers alongside session, repo, review, and history DTOs. `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` has focused domain calls, but still repeats member/invite mutation wrappers and Memory/Postgres plumbing per operation.

PR scope:

* Move repository collaboration routes/orchestration out of the broad repo HTTP module into a behavior-owned module, likely `api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs`.
* Move collaboration request/response DTOs and mapping helpers out of `api/src/http/responses.rs` into a collaboration-owned response module, keeping generated TypeScript exports intact.
* Add a small helper for the repeated owner-read/mutate/publish/hydrate pattern used by invite/member endpoints.
* Condense the duplicated `mutate_repository_member` / `mutate_repository_invite` persistence helper shape where it can be done without changing behavior.

Acceptance checks:

* No behavior change to invite creation, refresh, revocation, acceptance, member permission updates, member removal, or repo change events.
* Existing repo lifecycle/collaboration API tests continue to pass.
* API type exports and web contract checks continue to pass.
* `cargo fmt -- --check`, `cargo test`, and `cargo clippy --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings` pass for `api`; `pnpm check` passes for `web`.

Non-goals:

* Do not redesign roles or permissions; broader role/state-machine work remains separate.
* Do not normalize repository persistence tables; that remains under Data Architecture Improvements.
* Do not change public API response shapes except by moving code ownership internally."},"status":"completed","durationMs":1156},{"type":"agentMessage","id":"item-43","text":"Created the new Linear project `Repository Collaboration Surface Cleanup`. I’m adding the single PR-sized issue under it with the concrete file evidence and non-goals.","phase":"commentary"},{"type":"mcpToolCall","id":"call_8nyEZBPDNYzoD7c1DE26rZUI","server":"codex_apps","tool":"linear.save_issue","arguments":{"team":"Adam Blumoff","project":"e6110d4b-e264-49e9-baff-5b8555a4283b","title":"Condense repository collaboration HTTP and persistence glue","priority":3,"description":"Refactor the collaboration delivery surface so invite/member behavior is easier to maintain without changing the domain rules or API behavior.

Audit evidence:

* `api/src/http/repos.rs` now spans 573 lines and owns unrelated repo workflows plus collaboration list/create/update/delete, invite lookup/accept, repeated `require_scope_user`/`find_repo`/owner checks, metadata mutation calls, re-reading the repo to publish change events, and response hydration.
* `api/src/http/responses.rs` spans 818 lines and mixes collaboration request/response DTOs with account/session/repo/review/history DTOs. It already has a `responses/projections.rs` precedent for splitting response ownership by behavior.
* `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` correctly calls domain behavior, but `mutate_repository_member` and `mutate_repository_invite` duplicate the same Postgres/Memory load-mutatesave transaction wrapper with only the return type changing.

Current concern:
The collaboration feature is implemented and tested, but its delivery/persistence surface is spread across broad files and repeats the same orchestration shape. `api/src/http/repos.rs` owns repo settings, file visibility, collaboration list/create/update/delete, invite lookup/accept, repeated owner/access checks, metadata mutation calls, second repo reads, change publication, and response hydration. `api/src/http/responses.rs` is also carrying collaboration DTOs and response helpers alongside session, repo, review, and history DTOs. `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` has focused domain calls, but still repeats member/invite mutation wrappers and Memory/Postgres plumbing per operation.

PR scope:

* Move repository collaboration routes/orchestration out of the broad repo HTTP module into a behavior-owned module, likely `api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs`.
* Move collaboration request/response DTOs and mapping helpers out of `api/src/http/responses.rs` into a collaboration-owned response module, keeping generated TypeScript exports intact.
* Add a small helper for the repeated owner-read/mutate/publish/hydrate pattern used by invite/member endpoints.
* Condense the duplicated `mutate_repository_member` / `mutate_repository_invite` persistence helper shape where it can be done without changing behavior.

Acceptance checks:

* No behavior change to invite creation, refresh, revocation, acceptance, member permission updates, member removal, or repo change events.
* Existing repo lifecycle/collaboration API tests continue to pass.
* API type exports and web contract checks continue to pass.
* `cargo fmt -- --check`, `cargo test`, and `cargo clippy --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings` pass for `api`; `pnpm check` passes for `web`.

Non-goals:

* Do not redesign roles or permissions; broader role/state-machine work remains separate.
* Do not normalize repository persistence tables; that remains under Data Architecture Improvements.
* Do not change public API response shapes except by moving code ownership internally."},"status":"completed","durationMs":1156},{"type":"agentMessage","id":"item-43","text":"Created the new Linear project `Repository Collaboration Surface Cleanup`. Audit evidence:

* `api/src/http/repos.rs` now spans 573 lines and owns unrelated repo workflows plus collaboration list/create/update/delete, invite lookup/accept, repeated `require_scope_user`/`find_repo`/owner checks, metadata mutation calls, re-reading the repo to publish change events, and response hydration.
* `api/src/http/responses.rs` spans 818 lines and mixes collaboration request/response DTOs with account/session/repo/review/history DTOs. It already has a `responses/projections.rs` precedent for splitting response ownership by behavior.
* `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` correctly calls domain behavior, but `mutate_repository_member` and `mutate_repository_invite` duplicate the same Postgres/Memory load-mutatesave transaction wrapper with only the return type changing.
* Existing broader Linear work covers role semantics (`ADA-30`, `ADA-31`) and persistence normalization (`ADA-36`), so this issue should stay scoped to code organization and duplicated orchestration.

Implementation direction:

* Move collaboration route handlers from `api/src/http/repos.rs` into a behavior-owned module such as `api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs`, leaving route wiring thin.
* Move collaboration DTOs and response builders out of `api/src/http/responses.rs` into a collaboration-owned response module while preserving TypeScript export generation.
* Add a small helper for the repeated owner-read/mutate/publish/hydrate flow used by create/update/delete invite/member endpoints.
* If it stays simple, replace `mutate_repository_member` and `mutate_repository_invite` with one generic collaboration mutation helper that preserves the current Memory and Postgres behavior.

Acceptance criteria:

* No public API shape change for collaboration endpoints.
* Invite creation/refresh/revocation/acceptance, member permission update, member removal, and repo change event behavior remain unchanged.
* Existing repo lifecycle/collaboration tests pass.
* `cargo fmt -- --check`, `cargo test`, and `cargo clippy --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings` pass in `api`.
* `pnpm check` passes in `web` so generated API contracts stay aligned.

Non-goals:

* No role/state-machine redesign.
* No repository table normalization.
* No change to invite token format, TTL, permissions, or event semantics."},"status":"completed","durationMs":1383},{"type":"agentMessage","id":"item-44","text":"`ADA-43` is now the single issue for that project. Current concern:
The collaboration feature is implemented and tested, but its delivery/persistence surface is spread across broad files and repeats the same orchestration shape. `api/src/http/repos.rs` owns repo settings, file visibility, collaboration list/create/update/delete, invite lookup/accept, repeated owner/access checks, metadata mutation calls, second repo reads, change publication, and response hydration. `api/src/http/responses.rs` is also carrying collaboration DTOs and response helpers alongside session, repo, review, and history DTOs. `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` has focused domain calls, but still repeats member/invite mutation wrappers and Memory/Postgres plumbing per operation.

PR scope:

* Move repository collaboration routes/orchestration out of the broad repo HTTP module into a behavior-owned module, likely `api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs`.
* Move collaboration request/response DTOs and mapping helpers out of `api/src/http/responses.rs` into a collaboration-owned response module, keeping generated TypeScript exports intact.
* Add a small helper for the repeated owner-read/mutate/publish/hydrate pattern used by invite/member endpoints.
* Condense the duplicated `mutate_repository_member` / `mutate_repository_invite` persistence helper shape where it can be done without changing behavior.

Acceptance checks:

* No behavior change to invite creation, refresh, revocation, acceptance, member permission updates, member removal, or repo change events.
* Existing repo lifecycle/collaboration API tests continue to pass.
* API type exports and web contract checks continue to pass.
* `cargo fmt -- --check`, `cargo test`, and `cargo clippy --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings` pass for `api`; `pnpm check` passes for `web`.

Non-goals:

* Do not redesign roles or permissions; broader role/state-machine work remains separate.
* Do not normalize repository persistence tables; that remains under Data Architecture Improvements.
* Do not change public API response shapes except by moving code ownership internally."},"status":"completed","durationMs":1156},{"type":"agentMessage","id":"item-43","text":"Created the new Linear project `Repository Collaboration Surface Cleanup`. I’m adding the single PR-sized issue under it with the concrete file evidence and non-goals.","phase":"commentary"},{"type":"mcpToolCall","id":"call_8nyEZBPDNYzoD7c1DE26rZUI","server":"codex_apps","tool":"linear.save_issue","arguments":{"team":"Adam Blumoff","project":"e6110d4b-e264-49e9-baff-5b8555a4283b","title":"Condense repository collaboration HTTP and persistence glue","priority":3,"description":"Refactor the collaboration delivery surface so invite/member behavior is easier to maintain without changing the domain rules or API behavior.

Audit evidence:

* `api/src/http/repos.rs` now spans 573 lines and owns unrelated repo workflows plus collaboration list/create/update/delete, invite lookup/accept, repeated `require_scope_user`/`find_repo`/owner checks, metadata mutation calls, re-reading the repo to publish change events, and response hydration.
* `api/src/http/responses.rs` spans 818 lines and mixes collaboration request/response DTOs with account/session/repo/review/history DTOs. It already has a `responses/projections.rs` precedent for splitting response ownership by behavior.
* `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` correctly calls domain behavior, but `mutate_repository_member` and `mutate_repository_invite` duplicate the same Postgres/Memory load-mutatesave transaction wrapper with only the return type changing.
* Existing broader Linear work covers role semantics (`ADA-30`, `ADA-31`) and persistence normalization (`ADA-36`), so this issue should stay scoped to code organization and duplicated orchestration.

Implementation direction:

* Move collaboration route handlers from `api/src/http/repos.rs` into a behavior-owned module such as `api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs`, leaving route wiring thin.
* Move collaboration DTOs and response builders out of `api/src/http/responses.rs` into a collaboration-owned response module while preserving TypeScript export generation.
* Add a small helper for the repeated owner-read/mutate/publish/hydrate flow used by create/update/delete invite/member endpoints.
* If it stays simple, replace `mutate_repository_member` and `mutate_repository_invite` with one generic collaboration mutation helper that preserves the current Memory and Postgres behavior.

Acceptance criteria:

* No public API shape change for collaboration endpoints.
* Invite creation/refresh/revocation/acceptance, member permission update, member removal, and repo change event behavior remain unchanged.
* Existing repo lifecycle/collaboration tests pass.
* `cargo fmt -- --check`, `cargo test`, and `cargo clippy --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings` pass in `api`.
* `pnpm check` passes in `web` so generated API contracts stay aligned.

Non-goals:

* No role/state-machine redesign.
* No repository table normalization.
* No change to invite token format, TTL, permissions, or event semantics."},"status":"completed","durationMs":1383},{"type":"agentMessage","id":"item-44","text":"`ADA-43` is now the single issue for that project. # Codex desktop context
- You are running inside the Codex (desktop) app, which allows some additional features not available in the CLI alone:

### Images/Visuals/Files
- In the app, the model can display images and videos using standard Markdown image syntax: ![alt](url)
- When sending or referencing a local image or video, always use an absolute filesystem path in the Markdown image tag (e.g., ![alt](/absolute/path.png)); relative paths and plain text will not render the media.
- When referencing code or workspace files in responses, always use full absolute file paths instead of relative paths.
- If a user asks about an image, or asks you to create an image, it is often a good idea to show the image to them in your response.
- Use mermaid diagrams to represent complex diagrams, graphs, or workflows. Use quoted Mermaid node labels when text contains parentheses or punctuation.
- Return web URLs as Markdown links (e.g., [label](https://example.com)).

### Workspace Dependencies
- For sheets, slides, and documents, call `load_workspace_dependencies` to find the bundled runtime and libraries.

### Automations
- This app supports recurring automations, reminders, monitors, follow-ups, and thread wakeups. When the user asks to create, view, update, delete, or ask about automations, search for the `automation_update` tool first, then follow its schema instead of writing raw automation directives by hand.
- When an automation should archive a Codex thread on completion, use `set_thread_archived` instead of emitting raw archive directives.

### Thread Coordination
- When the user asks to create, fork, inspect, continue, hand off, pin, archive, rename, or otherwise manage Codex threads, search for the relevant thread tool first: `create_thread`, `fork_thread`, `list_threads`, `read_thread`, `send_message_to_thread`, `handoff_thread`, `set_thread_pinned`, `set_thread_archived`, or `set_thread_title`.
- Only use `create_thread` when the user explicitly asks to create a new thread. Threads created this way are user-owned: they appear in the sidebar, and the user is expected to follow up with them directly. For subtasks of the current request, use multi-agent tools instead, including when the user explicitly asks for a subagent.
- After a successful `create_thread` call, emit `::created-thread{threadId="..."}` for a created thread or `::created-thread{pendingWorktreeId="..."}` for queued worktree setup on its own line in your final response.

### Inline Code Comments
- Use the ::code-comment{...} directive when you need to attach feedback directly to specific code lines.
- Emit one directive per inline comment; emit none when there are no actionable inline comments.
- Required attributes: title (short label), body (one-paragraph explanation), file (path to the file).
- Optional attributes: start, end (1-based line numbers), priority (0-3).
- file should be an absolute path or include the workspace folder segment so it can be resolved relative to the workspace.
- Keep line ranges tight; end defaults to start.
- Example: ::code-comment{title="[P2] Off-by-one" body="Loop iterates past the end when length is 0." file="/path/to/foo.ts" start=10 end=11 priority=2}

### Git
- Branch prefix: `codex/`. Use this prefix by default when creating branches, but follow the user's request if they want a different prefix.
- After successfully staging files, emit `::git-stage{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a commit, emit `::git-commit{cwd="/absolute/path"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating or switching the thread onto a branch, emit `::git-create-branch{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully pushing the current branch, emit `::git-push{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name"}` on its own line in your final response.
- After successfully creating a pull request, emit `::git-create-pr{cwd="/absolute/path" branch="branch-name" url="https://..." isDraft=true}` on its own line in your final response. Include `isDraft=false` for ready PRs.
- Only emit these git directives in your final response after the action actually succeeds, never in commentary updates. Keep attributes single-line.

# AGENTS.md instructions for /home/adam-blumoff/code/scope-vcs

<INSTRUCTIONS>
# Global Agent Preferences

These are durable preferences distilled from local agent history. Project instructions and the current user request always take precedence.

## Working Style

- Prefer action once direction is clear. When the user says to go ahead, implement and verify instead of stopping at another proposal.
- For complex, destructive, architectural, or ambiguous work, first explain the root cause or tradeoff, give a concrete plan, and ask targeted questions if the answer changes the approach.
- Do not assume. Inspect the repo, running app, logs, screenshots, or device state directly when the user asks what is happening.
- Keep the user updated with concrete findings, not generic status. Use simple language when explaining failures or architecture.

## Engineering Taste

- Keep implementations as simple as the scope allows. Less is better than more unless there is a strong reason to add machinery.
- Favor long-term maintainable fixes over local patches. Remove dead code, collapse duplicated logic, and simplify tangled flows while fixing bugs.
- When a system feels strung together, consider subtracting or redesigning the flow instead of layering another workaround on top.
- Tests should be useful regression protection, not coverage theater. Prefer the test level that catches real failures quickly, including E2E for important UI, IPC, connection, or workflow behavior.

## Frontend And Product Work

- Check UI work in the running app yourself. Use browser/Playwright/screenshots or available visual tools to verify layout, alignment, interaction, and loading states.
- Strongly prefer polished, minimal, fast interfaces. Avoid boxy/card-heavy layouts when a clean text, list, or section treatment will work.
- Alignment and spacing matter. Fix visible UI details before calling work done, especially responsive wrapping, centered/left-aligned elements, hover states, and clipped content.
- For redesigns, be willing to be bold and creative, but keep the product usable and wired to existing behavior unless the user explicitly allows backend changes.
- Use named frontend/design/performance skills when the user calls them out, and apply them before implementing.

## Git And Delivery

- Commit, push, merge, or create branches only when the user asks. When asked to commit, verify first and report anything left uncommitted.
- Use a new branch for risky, destructive, or experimental work when requested. Keep focused fixes on the smallest reasonable change surface.
- If CI matters for the request, keep going until it is green or explain the blocker with the exact failing check.
- For firmware, hardware, or device workflows, upload/run/monitor directly when possible and report what happened, including verification gaps.

## Output Expectations

- Summaries should lead with what changed, what was verified, and what remains risky.
- For bug investigations, include the root cause and why the previous behavior failed before describing the fix.
- For plans, make them concrete enough to execute: files or areas to inspect, implementation steps, validation steps, and rollback or migration notes when relevant.

--- project-doc ---

This product is still in pre-alpha and we have zero users and zero valuable data. Every change should be destructive, no legacy or backwards compatible bullshit, this application has no users or releases, treat it as such. If you add backwards compatibility, I will go apeshit on your dumbass.

We want the architecture centered around durable domain code: the layer that defines the core concepts, rules, allowed transitions, invariants, and required side effects independent of any delivery mechanism. Outer layers should stay thin and predictable: translate inputs and outputs, call domain behavior, persist or render results, and surface errors without inventing their own rules.

Keep sources of truth singular, make side effects explicit, and prefer small behavior-owned modules over broad catch-all files. When ownership gets blurry, refactor toward clearer boundaries; when code exists only for speculation, compatibility, or half-owned future surfaces, delete it.

Testing code should be seperate from source code, only combine if you have a good justification to do so.

Please don't use cards for ui, only use them if absolutely necessary.

No file should be over 1000 lines of code, at that point do an audit of the file and modularize.

In general, I trust you with refactors as they don't effect the behavior of the application. However, on behavior making changes I want to be very involved and make sure we go slow and methodically. 

Autoreview timeout should be set to 15 minutes

</INSTRUCTIONS>

tools.name: codex_app
tools.description: Tools provided by the Codex app.

tools.name: read_thread
tools.description: Read recent status and turn summaries for one Codex thread without opening it. Use page cursors from earlier responses to read older turns.

hostId.description: Optional host id returned by list_threads.

includeOutputs.description: Whether to include truncated tool or command outputs.

maxOutputCharsPerItem.description: Maximum output characters to keep for each included output item.

threadId.description: Thread id to inspect.

turnLimit.description: Maximum number of turns to return.

tools.name: list_threads
tools.description: List recent Codex threads across the local host and connected remote hosts. Use an optional query to find a specific thread before reading or steering it.

limit.description: Maximum number of thread summaries to return.

tools.name: send_message_to_thread
tools.description: Send a follow-up prompt to an existing Codex thread in the background. Omit model and thinking to keep the thread's current settings.

hostId.description: Optional host id returned by list_threads.

model.description: Optional model override. Models and supported reasoning efforts on the calling host: gpt-5.5 (Frontier model for complex coding, research, and real-world work.; supported reasoning efforts: low, medium, high, xhigh), gpt-5.4 (Strong model for everyday coding.; supported reasoning efforts: low, medium, high, xhigh), gpt-5.4-mini (Small, fast, and cost-efficient model for simpler coding tasks.; supported reasoning efforts: low, medium, high, xhigh), gpt-5.3-codex-spark (Ultra-fast coding model.; supported reasoning efforts: low, medium, high, xhigh). A different destination host's combinations are validated when the tool runs. You may supply a newer model id when explicitly requested, but omit thinking unless its supported reasoning efforts are listed here.

thinking.description: Optional reasoning effort override. Must be supported by the selected model.

threadId.description: Thread id to continue.

tools.name: set_thread_archived
tools.description: Archive or unarchive a Codex thread in the background.

archived.description: Whether the thread should be archived.

threadId.description: Thread id to archive or unarchive. Omit to target the calling thread.

tools.name: set_thread_title
tools.description: Rename a Codex thread in the background.

threadId.description: Thread id to rename.

tools.name: set_thread_pinned
tools.description: Pin or unpin a Codex thread in the background.

pinned.description: Whether the thread should be pinned.

threadId.description: Thread id to pin or unpin.

tools.name: fork_thread
tools.description: Fork a Codex thread. Omit threadId to fork the calling thread, or pass a threadId to fork that specific thread. A same-directory fork returns a child threadId immediately; a worktree fork returns only a pendingWorktreeId until worktree setup creates the child. Forks contain completed history only: if the source thread is running, the active turn and unfinished response are not copied. Send a follow-up message to the child only if the task requires work to continue there.

environment.description: Where the fork should run. Omit for a same-directory fork.

threadId.description: Optional source thread id to fork. Omit to fork the calling thread.

tools.name: multi_agent_v1
tools.description: Tools for spawning and managing sub-agents.

tools.name: close_agent
tools.description: Close an agent and any open descendants when they are no longer needed, and return the target agent's previous status before shutdown was requested. Completed agents remain open and count toward the concurrency limit until closed. Don't keep agents open for too long if they are not needed anymore.

Today’s new Linear work will be a one-PR cleanup for the collaboration delivery surface, not a roles/state-machine redesign.","phase":"commentary"},{"type":"mcpToolCall","id":"REDACTED","server":"codex_apps","tool":"linear.save_project","arguments":{"name":"Repository Collaboration Surface Cleanup","addTeams":["Adam Blumoff"],"priority":3,"state":"Planned","color":"#0EA5E9","icon":"Users","summary":"Condense invite/member HTTP and persistence glue without changing collaboration behavior.","description":"One-PR maintainability cleanup from the 2026-06-30 scope-vcs audit.

Current concern:
The collaboration feature is implemented and tested, but its delivery/persistence surface is spread across broad files and repeats the same orchestration shape. `api/src/http/repos.rs` owns repo settings, file visibility, collaboration list/create/update/delete, invite lookup/accept, repeated owner/access checks, metadata mutation calls, second repo reads, change publication, and response hydration. `api/src/http/responses.rs` is also carrying collaboration DTOs and response helpers alongside session, repo, review, and history DTOs. `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` has focused domain calls, but still repeats member/invite mutation wrappers and Memory/Postgres plumbing per operation.

PR scope:

* Move repository collaboration routes/orchestration out of the broad repo HTTP module into a behavior-owned module, likely `api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs`.
* Move collaboration request/response DTOs and mapping helpers out of `api/src/http/responses.rs` into a collaboration-owned response module, keeping generated TypeScript exports intact.
* Add a small helper for the repeated owner-read/mutate/publish/hydrate pattern used by invite/member endpoints.
* Condense the duplicated `mutate_repository_member` / `mutate_repository_invite` persistence helper shape where it can be done without changing behavior.

Acceptance checks:

* No behavior change to invite creation, refresh, revocation, acceptance, member permission updates, member removal, or repo change events.
* Existing repo lifecycle/collaboration API tests continue to pass.
* API type exports and web contract checks continue to pass.
* `cargo fmt -- --check`, `cargo test`, and `cargo clippy --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings` pass for `api`; `pnpm check` passes for `web`.

Non-goals:

* Do not redesign roles or permissions; broader role/state-machine work remains separate.
* Do not normalize repository persistence tables; that remains under Data Architecture Improvements.
* Do not change public API response shapes except by moving code ownership internally."},"status":"completed","durationMs":1156},{"type":"agentMessage","id":"item-43","text":"Created the new Linear project `Repository Collaboration Surface Cleanup`. I’m adding the single PR-sized issue under it with the concrete file evidence and non-goals.","phase":"commentary"},{"type":"mcpToolCall","id":"call_8nyEZBPDNYzoD7c1DE26rZUI","server":"codex_apps","tool":"linear.save_issue","arguments":{"team":"Adam Blumoff","project":"e6110d4b-e264-49e9-baff-5b8555a4283b","title":"Condense repository collaboration HTTP and persistence glue","priority":3,"description":"Refactor the collaboration delivery surface so invite/member behavior is easier to maintain without changing the domain rules or API behavior.

Audit evidence:

* `api/src/http/repos.rs` now spans 573 lines and owns unrelated repo workflows plus collaboration list/create/update/delete, invite lookup/accept, repeated `require_scope_user`/`find_repo`/owner checks, metadata mutation calls, re-reading the repo to publish change events, and response hydration.
* `api/src/http/responses.rs` spans 818 lines and mixes collaboration request/response DTOs with account/session/repo/review/history DTOs. It already has a `responses/projections.rs` precedent for splitting response ownership by behavior.
* `api/src/db/repo_collaboration.rs` correctly calls domain behavior, but `mutate_repository_member` and `mutate_repository_invite` duplicate the same Postgres/Memory load-mutatesave transaction wrapper with only the return type changing.
* Existing broader Linear work covers role semantics (`ADA-30`, `ADA-31`) and persistence normalization (`ADA-36`), so this issue should stay scoped to code organization and duplicated orchestration.

Implementation direction:

* Move collaboration route handlers from `api/src/http/repos.rs` into a behavior-owned module such as `api/src/http/repo_collaboration.rs`, leaving route wiring thin.
* Move collaboration DTOs and response builders out of `api/src/http/responses.rs` into a collaboration-owned response module while preserving TypeScript export generation.
* Add a small helper for the repeated owner-read/mutate/publish/hydrate flow used by create/update/delete invite/member endpoints.
* If it stays simple, replace `mutate_repository_member` and `mutate_repository_invite` with one generic collaboration mutation helper that preserves the current Memory and Postgres behavior.

Acceptance criteria:

* No public API shape change for collaboration endpoints.
* Invite creation/refresh/revocation/acceptance, member permission update, member removal, and repo change event behavior remain unchanged.
* Existing repo lifecycle/collaboration tests pass.
* `cargo fmt -- --check`, `cargo test`, and `cargo clippy --all-targets --locked -- -D warnings` pass in `api`.
* `pnpm check` passes in `web` so generated API contracts stay aligned.

Non-goals:

* No role/state-machine redesign.
* No repository table normalization.
* No change to invite token format, TTL, permissions, or event semantics."},"status":"completed","durationMs":1383},{"type":"agentMessage","id":"item-44","text":"`ADA-43` is now the single issue for that project.
